ocar.opportunity: Dense connectomics reconstruction from large EM volumes requires annotating terabytes of data, but existing annotation tools were desktop-only applications requiring local data storage and limiting collaborative multi-user workflows.
ocar.challenge: Providing interactive 3D annotation of multi-terabyte EM datasets to distributed teams of proofreaders requires streaming large volumes efficiently through a browser interface while maintaining annotation consistency across concurrent users.
ocar.action: The authors developed webKnossos, a web-based platform for 3D EM data annotation that streams tiled, multi-resolution image data to browsers and provides tools for skeleton tracing, collaborative proofreading, and dataset management.
ocar.resolution: webKnossos enabled efficient online annotation of connectomics datasets at scale, supporting crowdsourced proofreading workflows and reducing annotation time through task management and quality control features. It was deployed for large-scale connectomics projects and is now widely used in the field.
ocar.future_work: Future development includes deeper integration with automated segmentation pipelines, AI-assisted annotation suggestions, and support for multimodal datasets combining EM with fluorescence channels.

summaries.beginner: Mapping neural connections from microscope images requires many people to manually trace nerve cells through thousands of image slices. webKnossos is a website that lets teams of annotators work together online to trace neurons in 3D, making large projects much more manageable.
summaries.intermediate: webKnossos is a web-based platform designed for efficient collaborative annotation of large 3D EM connectomics datasets. Its streaming tile architecture handles multi-terabyte volumes in real time while task management tools enable coordinated workflows for distributed annotation teams. It has been adopted for several major connectomics projects and integrates with automated segmentation pipelines.
summaries.advanced: webKnossos implements a hierarchical tile-streaming architecture that delivers multi-resolution 3D EM volumes to web browsers with sub-second latency, supporting both skeleton-based neurite tracing and volumetric annotation. Task management features enable quality-controlled, coordinated distributed proofreading. A key design decision is the separation of annotation from data storage, allowing large datasets to remain server-side. Limitations include reliance on skeleton (centerline) rather than mesh-based representations, which can lose morphological detail, though recent updates have added volumetric segmentation support.

discussion_prompts: What are the trade-offs between skeleton-based and volume-based annotation approaches in webKnossos for different connectomics use cases?
How does webKnossos handle annotation consistency and conflict resolution when multiple users proofread the same neuron segment simultaneously?
What metrics would you use to evaluate the throughput and accuracy improvements that webKnossos provides over previous desktop annotation tools?
